Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-2---The-Worl.content:
Design Notes
General Bestiary Template
[T/N: Laid out roughly in order. See other finished samples for finished product examples.]
An entry’s Description, Wants, Intellect & Morality are invokable Facts.

































KeyValueStatsHD [[HD]+[Level]] ([HP] + [Armor] AP), BR @, Size @’, Weight @#, Max. Load @#, Run @’, Fly @’, Swim @’, Dig @‘Group@ / @ / @ [T/N: Group size, as a set of possible encounter sizes, in ascending size. i.e. Solo, group/pack/murder/sounder, warband/horde.]Desc.[T/N: VERY brief physical description. 2 goals: cover anything that isn’t obvious to a GM by name/convention & provide tactile interesting fragmentary terms to bolster descriptions. no more than 200 characters.]Wants[T/N: As terse a description of the actor’s core interests as possible. i.e. golem: “Follow commandments to the letter & no further.” wolf: “Food, shelter, territory. The pack worships Tanto.“]Intellect[T/N: Terse, colorful assessment of intelligence. Clarification & instructions on handling alien neurology or behavior only if required/non-obvious. “Spells are like animals that can interpret simple speech.” “Brilliant only at solving riddles & idiotic otherwise.“]Morality[T/N: Terse, colorful assessment of morality. “Opportunistic savagery.” “Ludicrously & comically malevolent.” “Well-intentioned foppery.“]


                  
                  Translator's notes 
                  
                

HD & HP
HD is physical & spiritual durability. Humans are 2 HD & harpies, salamanders, & Coralers are all 1 HD. Otherwise, we stick kinda close to OSR, but maybe add 1 HD to normal assumptions. All HD are d6. HD ½ = 2 HP & halved rolls. HD 0 = always 0 HP. this isn’t lethal but it’s instant death if you take a hit. HP is always (HD × 4).
Level
We assume Level is 0 (& don’t bother writing it down) unless this entry is SPECIFICALLY for a ruler of a Faction (i.e. it’s a “Duke” or “General” or “Business Magnate” entry) in which case defer to core rules’ Level table to determine average assumed size.
Armor (AP)
Armor indicates absorption via Armor Points. Strictly speaking, Armor Points is a multiple of Power (HD + Level) added as an additional HP-equivalent & is based on the quality of gear. Leather is Power × 1, Chain is × 2, Plate is × 4. We can conceivably have bigger Armor bonuses for better Armor materials … but probably not.
That said, we don’t show the Armor multiplier value since it can be worked out from just the final AP number. To get the original Armor number, you can divide AP by Power.
Entities that have high HP but low HD (i.e. they are of weak character/strength of mind) should have an Armor bonus instead of getting extra HD. In addition, beasts that are literally heavily armored (like dragons) should have AP. We’d call this natural armor but we have no reason to make a distinction. In mechanical terms, some attacks can pierce Armor & attack HP directly & Armor provided from gear doesn’t heal from rest. Those are the main practical differences.
Calculating BR
As per the rules in Battles, under Battle Rating (p. @).
Size
Size is the cylindrical area you can fit into while fighting. Any value over 5’ should be rounded to 5’ increments. Standard sizes are 0’, 1’, 2’, 5’, 10’, 15’, 20’, 30’, 40’, & 60’. Arbitrarily larger sizes are also acceptable.
Weight & Max. Load
Weight is measured in stone (#). 1# = 10lb. We accept ½#, ¼#, & N/A. Should generally be rounded to increments of 3# where possible. Weight should reflect realistic mass (i.e. “this thing is 1,000 pounds” = it should be Weight 100#). Max. Load is carry capacity & should generally be 2/3 of Weight unless your knowledge of the creature’s biology for some reason makes an exception worthwhile.
Speed scores
Run, Fly, Swim & Dig (in that order) should be in 5’ increments wherever possible. Don’t list Speeds that a creature doesn’t have. Humans don’t have a Dig score. Possessing a score indicates a natural capacity for it; Humans can swim, but they don’t have Swim 15’. Climbing (i.e. like a spider) is not a separate score; it is a Feature that lets you use Run to climb. Humans move at Run 25’. We treat 5’ as 1” on a combat surface normally.
Weapon & attack design philosophy
Classic B/X or OSR design gives various monsters a bunch of redundant/similar attacks. bite+bite+claw? nigga pls, they all do Slash damage. A weapon is meaningfully differentiated if its damage, type, usage patterns, or special effects (i.e. range, armor pierce, burning) matter. if there’s no reason to ever choose between two different weapons (natural or otherwise), why separate them?
All weapons are phrased as Features tagged with (Weapon). Natural weapons are tagged (Natural Weapon). Weapons are implicitly melee unless a range is provided (in feet, or as “LoS” for line-of-sight effects).
Weapons usually don’t grant multiple Strikes except as particularly special abilities. Extra Strikes are a separate Feature & most natural weapons should be combined into one big weapon if they have no differentiation.



Feature 1: Description of Feature. [T/N: Features can be stolen by players who cook & eat the monster. Keep that in mind while designing them. features should be simple enough that a paragraph of text fully describes their mechanics. if that’s not enough space for whatever weird shit this thing does, add it to the description & include whatever fancy tools you need i.e. md tables there.]



                  
                  T/N: Feature design philosophy 
                  
                


Simplicity over fidelity. Avoid fiddly conditionals or mental spoon load. Strive for obvious/mnemonic conditions if you need conditionals.
Avoid referencing specific weaponry unless it’s essential to the Feature. (“Natural weapons” is better than “claws”; think like, if a PC gets this power, would it make no sense? sometimes i.e. mind flayer tentacles, it’s unavoidable and that’s okay. other times, you can correct for it.)
If it only works in rare circumstances or just grants a +whatever bonus to niche actions, consider cutting it or merging it with another Feature. Each Feature should be ideally punchy and central to the thing’s identity.



[T/N: Description goes here. Very brief text description of whatever is even worth mentioning that you haven’t already. We don’t really talk about ecology or all the gay bullshit AD&D liked to bloat its page count with. Anything you put here should be witty, terse, and/or very highly relevant to what a panicked GM flipping to this page might want to know about the creature that isn’t already covered. No more than 1-2 sentences unless you really have to include a bunch of rules text that can’t be a Feature (i.e. because players should NEVER be able to get it) or if you want to do something fun like mention that you can re-use an animated snowman’s hat to animate other snow things or whatever the entry demands.]
Treasure: [Only if any consistent treasures to note; otherwise, N/A. Entries that demand interesting/legendary treasures may have a reference to a table i.e. “See table.” plus adjacent table(s) of treasures.]


                  
                  T/N: Optional: GM Prep Tables 
                  
                

Determine the most difficult part of using the creature. Basically, ask yourself “what’s the hardest thing for a GM to make up on the spot that will almost always be needed with this creature?” pick the top 0-3. Sphinxes need riddles & GMs never have a book of riddles on-hand. We want to maximize utility. a prep table should NEVER consist of:

Mundanities (the dwarf has a family)
easy stereotypes (the dwarf is a miner with a beard & is named greyhammer & lives in a hill)
random bullshit (the dwarf is a gay cake-maker)

it should be structured, difficult-to-ad-hoc content with extremely high conceptual density. whatever’s critical to its identity & hard to come up with. “Do the hard work for the GM.”
some ideas: “What is this thing doing?” “What is its nature?” “Cause/casus belli for [stupid shit they do but that’s difficult to wrap your head around as a human who doesn’t think like they do]”. victims/slaves/captives, symbiotes/parasites & other common/universal relationships, “why the fuck would we ever bother this thing/who’s prompting us into it & why”, “common/interesting variants or very similar creatures you can make by changing this thing’s name & giving it some minor tweaks that you can contain in 160-200 characters”, “organizational structure”, “premade encounters”, “hazards that are required to go along with this entity to make it work conceptually”, “weird weapons & armor”, “WHY the FUCK does this thing EXIST, who did this and why???”, “set of specific highly interesting & thematically appropriate loot”, “legendary/mythical powers associated with this creature or with special/unique versions of it”.
write tables as proper Markdown tables. don’t bother giving them headings since you can just put the heading into the table itself like the examples below. you can include multiple columns if they all go together i.e. “Gem color” “Value” “Power” or whatever. we only use d6 dice, so 1d6 for most tables, or 2d6 if you need lots of results or weighted results. you can also use d66 numbered double per entry i.e. 11–12, 13–14 […] 65–66 to get an 18-entry table w/ a flat curve … but you’d better have a really exciting reason to make an 18-entry table.




                  
                  Optional: When eaten 
                  
                

Flavor: [Terse, colorful description of flavor or experience of consumption. “Disgustingly fatty pork.” “Like eating coal, because you are, and it’s just as unhealthy.“]
Notes: [Description of effects, either as a flat-out “this happens” or “Roll on table below.” followed by a table (1d6 or 2d6) of 2-4 side effects. Side effects should be equally balanced by permanence & positive/negative effects. They can just outright kill the players via poison or horrible nightmare magical fate if that’s appropriate for the creature in question. You can include qualifiers.]




                  
                  T/N: Regarding When Eaten entries 
                  
                

Players can eat monsters to gain better stats (if it had better stats than them) & their Features or Facts (as mutations, kinda). If a creature has other unusual effects or characteristics related to its consumption (i.e. some beasts are HORRIBLY CURSED or KNOWN TO CURE DISEASE or THE FEAST OF KINGS), that’s what this section is for. It should NOT be included for anything of blisteringly mundane quality. Basically, if you’re describing a normal meal, this section should not be here. A goat entry doesn’t deserve a When Eaten section (unless it’s a Scapegoat & eating it transfers all the awful sins the goat bears onto the consumer or some shit).
write tables as proper Markdown tables. don’t nest rolls inside them. Don’t use Saves, just do straight 1d6 or 2d6 tables with entries weighted appropriately.




                  
                  T/N: Other general design principles 
                  
                


Try not to use funny names where English words or calques would be better. Names should be mnemonic where possible; trivial to remember.
Natural weapons are not Features for the sake of BR. No weapon is.
We do some lumping. Basically, ask yourself if a creature could be achieved with a very minor edit to the stats of this creature. A name edit, a tweak to a stat or two, maybe one extra ability. The giant badger is a slight edit of the wolf. The dolphin is a slight edit of the seal.

Tables of variant creatures should never include the base creature. If a GM wanted to use the creature as-is, he’d just do that.


Generally speaking, pack hunters don’t need abilities that specifically represent their skill for hunting in packs; this is implicit to the way the Battles system works. A pack has a huge advantage in claiming tactical bonuses (terrain, perception, flanking, maneuverability, morale) which can potentially add up to a +4 collectively (on a 2d6 Save!). This could allow a relatively small pack to fight on even ground against a creature with up to 4× more BR than the pack does. Ergo, don’t give a “pack hunter” bonus unless it’s really, sincerely justified.
AP must always be granted by an Armor score. If you want to give a creature an ablative pool of HP somehow (i.e. a robot having parts with separate HP), you should give it fixed HP and/or HD.
Immunities, Resistances, and Weaknesses are conveyed via Features because we want them to be copiable by alchemy. (Every Feature is potentially an ability that a player could obtain.)
Unusual/magical equipment is listed as a Feature, tagged with (Equipment), i.e. “Magic Shield (Equipment)“.

Unusual/magical weapons are similarly tagged as {Weapon}.
Unusual natural weapons are tagged as (Natural Weapon).
Class Features (i.e. derived from profession or knowledge) are labeled (Class) & are otherwise presumed to be Racial (or natural) Features.





Description, Wants, Intellect, & Morality can all be invoked as Facts where necessary.
The Wild Things
Bear

































KeyValueStatsHD 6 (24), BR 90, Size 10’, Weight 75#, Max. Load 50#, Run 35’, Swim 20’GroupSolo / mother & 1d6−2 cubsDesc.Brown shag, button eyes. Claws like knives, rotting breath & deep grunts.WantsFood. Territory. To be left alone. Cubs are sacred.IntellectAnimalistic but clever. Excellent memory for food, threats, grudges.MoralityNo malice, no mercy.

Teeth & Claws (Natural Weapon): 10 Physical.
Claws & Teeth: You get an extra Strike per Attack made with a natural weapon.
Mauling Embrace: If you strike an enemy twice in melee with natural weapons, you may immediately Grapple them.
Terrible Roar: Once per 10 minutes, may roar as a Free Action. All creatures of lesser Power within earshot must Save or Panic.

Can smell food from a mile away. Will investigate anything edible. A brown bear in your granary is a problem you solve not by fighting, but by moving.
Some Lords (“foolishly”) keep trained bears as status symbols.
Treasure: N/A





















1d6Bear variants1–2Black Bear. HD 3 (12), BR 45, Weight 25#. Always flees when able.3–4Cave Bear. HD 7 (28), BR 135, Weight 100#. Additional (3 total) Strike per Attack made with a natural weapon. Hides in the mountains.5–6Rim Bear. HD 7 (28), Weight 100#. Resistant to Ice Damage. Aggressive. White.

































1d6What is this bear doing?1Eating. Will defend food source. Beehive (angry bees), fish (at river), carrion (disease), or stolen human food (angry owner).2Hibernating in den. Will not wake unless physically disturbed or fire/smoke introduced.3Foraging with cubs. Maximum aggression. Will not stop until threat eliminated or cubs escape.4Marking territory. Clawing trees, rubbing. Not immediately hostile but will challenge if party doesn’t retreat.5Following a scent trail. May be tracking the Party’s food, or something else entirely that leads past them.6Wounded and irritable. Previous encounter with hunters, rival bear, or mountain imp trap. Attacks first.
Boar

































KeyValueStatsHD 2 (8), BR 5, Size 5’, Weight 18#, Max. Load 12#, Run 35’Group2d6 sounder / 3d6×10 warbandDesc.Thick filthy hide. Small eyes, poor vision. Exceptional sense of smell.WantsTo eat & wallow. To be left alone.IntellectAnimalistic. Can be domesticated, but never tamed.MoralityWanton pride, stubbornness & wrath.

Tusks (Natural Weapon): 5 Physical.
Gore Charge: You count as twice your Weight when performing a Charge attack.
Spiteful Stubbornness: If you are incapacitated or killed in melee combat, immediately make a free Strike against the perpetrator.

Hated by the Commons; a sounder can wreck a season’s harvest in a night. Loved by Lords; there’s nothing better than a boar at a feast.
Normally flees. If cornered/challenged, absurd commitment to violence. Defends sows and piglets to the death.
Treasure: N/A

































1d6Boar encounters1Rooting in forest. Will ignore Party unless approached within 20’.2Drinking at water source. Territorial. Will charge if party approaches water.3Fleeing from something worse — wolves, bear, hunters, or fire. Stampede through party’s location.4Lone boar, separated from sounder. Paranoid and aggressive.5Sow with piglets, no boar nearby. Will not attack first but will not retreat.6Already aggressive — recently wounded, encountered hunters, or in mating season. Attacks on sight.
Horse

































KeyValueStatsHD 3 (12), BR 4, Size 10’, Weight 90#, Max. Load 60#, Run 50’GroupSolo / 2d6 herd / 3d6 × 10 cavalryDesc.Long legs, alert ears. Glossy coat, nervous eyes. Snorts & whinnies when alarmed.WantsGrass, water, the herd. A rider who knows what they’re doing.IntellectRead emotion & intent. Can be trained extensively but panic easily.MoralityFlight before fight. Loyal to those who treat them well.

Hooves (Natural Weapon): 5 Physical.

House Beacon cavalry is renowned across the Inner Sea. The war has made a horse precious; every faction raids for them. Injured horses are eaten rather than healed.
Untrained horses always flee anything that seems dangerous.
Treasure: N/A





















1d6Horse variants1–2Draft horse. HD 4 (16), BR 5, Weight 150#, Max. Load 100#, Run 35’. Always flees from combat.3–4Warhorse. HD 4 (16), BR 5, Weight 120#, Max. Load 80#. Trained for combat; immune to Panic.5–6Steppe horse. Weight 60#, Max. Load 40#. Can survive on steppe grass alone.

































1d6Why is this horse alone?1Rider killed nearby. Body within 100’. Ambush possibly still present.2Escaped from bandits/army camp. Branded. Someone wants it back.3Wild mountain herd horse. Unbroken. Will flee.4Draft horse slipped its harness & hid. Overturned cart nearby. Owner searching.5Messenger horse whose rider vanished en route. Saddlebags contain important secrets from a presently-relevant Faction.6Decoy. Trained to lure horse-thieves into ambush.
Monkey

































KeyValueStatsHD 2 (8), BR 2, Size 5’, Weight 6#, Max. Load 4#, Run 25’Group1d6 + 1 small troop / 2d6 × 4 troopDesc.Ugly 3-foot-tall white/pale red-faced monkey with a tail. Horse-like grin with powerful jaws.WantsMonkey see, monkey want.IntellectVery intelligent (for an animal). Devious, brilliant schemer.MoralityNone, really.

Claws (Natural Weapon): 4 Physical.
Tree Climbing: You can climb trees & other sturdy flora as if flat ground using your Run score.
Monkey Brain: When executing a plan, you must Save or somehow ruin it by impulse (eating your own bait, falling for the trap yourself, getting into a fight with a co-conspirator).

The Glassian monkey tends to stay in the woods & stick to its troops.
Treasure: N/A








































1d6Monkey troop structureMonkey encounter1Tension from multiple competing males.Migrating, looking for new land.2All-male troop of rejects from other troops.Fleeing from apex predator; will evaluate & flee from hostility.3Females scrambling for a new leader.Patrolling territory; will intimidate & posture ritualistically.4Sole female; rest of troop is infants.Hungry from famine, drought, or calamity; seeking food. May become recurring pest.5Sole male leading a harem.In nesting grounds, confused; will move to intimidation to drive you out.6Leader & many loyal paired goons.Bound & escorted by mercenaries (p. @) or harpies (p. @).





















1d6Monkey variants1–2Golden. Glittering fur. Extremely rare. If touched or clawed, Save vs. greedy intent or petrify into a fool’s gold statue. If skinned, worth 2d6 × $10,000.3–4Chimpanzee. No Monkey Brain Feature. Warlike, mischevious, aggressively territorial. Capable of organized aggressive action.5–6Orangutan. Arboreal, intrinsically solitary. Pretends to wisdom, in a pantomime manner.
Tiger, Vanishing

































KeyValueStatsHD 5 (20), BR @, Size 10’, Weight 48#, Max. Load 32#, Run 35’GroupSolo / mother & 1d6−2 cubsDesc.Huge black & orange cat plastered with stripes. Cold yellow eyes.WantsProud grandeur; a thrilling hunt.IntellectSilent, but as cunning & ruthless as the most dedicated human hunters.MoralityBows only to greater strength. Catlike, sans mercy.

Vanishing Cat: Once per day as a Minor Action, turn invisible for an hour. This does not hide scent, sound, or other traces, but you are almost undetectable if still & enemies suffer a −2 to Strike Saves even if they can approximate your location.

Prone to logician’s fallacies.MoralityLaid-back & easy-going. Genuinely well-intentioned.

Fists (Natural Weapon): 2 Physical.
Moon Telepathy: You are Immune to Mind Damage. You can broadcast & receive speech as if talking to everyone with Moon Telepathy up to at most a 1-mile radius. You can dim the range or mute your ability to hear other broadcasts.
Bound: Once per Turn as a Free Action, leap up to 15’ in any direction.
Rabbit Instincts: You must Save to not Panic from unexpected loud noises, dogs, or eagles (outside ongoing combat). You must Save to not investigate interesting smells.

Unchanging aeons of relative ease in Lunar cities has divorced them from the realities of life. They know almost nothing about Aia & are astonished (& easily entranced) by stories of war, conquest, famine, charisma, tournaments, politics, monster-slaying, etc. They will follow interesting people around asking lots of questions & then broadcast the answers to everyone.
Moon rabbits are immortal. On the Moon, they have a half-life of around 500 years.
Treasure: Jade coins (3d6 × $10).

































1d6What they want from you1Stories about Aia. Will trade local information for entertainment. 3-in-6 chance they immediately tell everyone everything.2Help with task. Needs something fetched from dangerous/restricted area. Doesn’t understand why it’s dangerous. “Could you get this from the vault? I’m not allowed in there but YOU don’t work here so it should be fine.”3Vegetables from Aia. Will pay absurd amounts of jade coins for normal carrots.4Opinion on dispute. Two rabbits are having an argument that’s been going on for three months. They want outside perspective. The argument is deeply stupid.5Introduction to interesting person. Heard a rumor about someone (PC or NPC) and wants to meet them. Will drag you to them OR drag them to you, whichever is less convenient.6Nothing yet, but… Following you around because you’re the most interesting thing to happen in weeks. Broadcasting running commentary on everything you do to all nearby rabbits. The Royal Guards will hear within hours.

































1d6The useful information they can offer1Guard rotations: Knows when Royal Guards change shifts, which routes they take, which ones are more alert. Learned this through decades of watching.2Secret passages: “Oh, that wall? It’s not really a wall. You just push on the third crystal from the left.” Has no idea this is supposed to be secret.3Current gossip: Knows where everyone important is right now and what they’re doing. Updates in real-time via telepathy network.4Artifact locations: “That thing you’re looking for? I think it’s in the Eastern Vault. Or maybe the Archive? One of those buildings that glows purple.”5Cultural taboos: Can explain what will DEFINITELY get you caught. “Don’t touch the statues. The Princess gets really mad. Last time someone did that she petrified an entire village.”6How things work: Can explain lunar technology in terms that are technically accurate but utterly useless. “The door opens when it recognizes you. How does it recognize you? It looks at you. With its eyes. The crystal eyes. They’re in the frame.”

































1d6Rabbit problems1Lost something important. “I’ll get in trouble! I mean, it’s not that important, but it’s in a restricted area. Could you help me? I’ll help you get in if you’ll help me.”2Witnessed something unintended. Royal Guard moving artifact, classified gossip, mysterious break-in. Desperately want to tell people but I can’t, so I’m telling you.3Accidentally broke something. “Don’t tell my supervisor! I didn’t mean to! I don’t care if it’s tiny! Help me replace it, or get him to stop looking or something!“4In love with someone inappropriate. Another rabbit, a Royal Guard, possibly one of the PCs. Their telepathic yearning is embarrassing everyone.5Caught between orders. “I can’t do A because then I’m not doing B, but I can’t do B because then I’m not doing A!“6Bored beyond measure. Has done the same job for 300 years. Will absolutely help you do anything illegal if it’s interesting. Doesn’t understand consequences because nothing consequential has happened to them ever.

































1d6Rabbit jobs @1Mochi production specialist. Pounds rice all day. Very strong opinions about mochi texture.2@3Agricultural tender. Grows the deeply weird lunar crops. @4Archive assistant. Files paperwork that no one will ever read. Has access to restricted information but doesn’t realize it’s restricted.5Messenger runner. Carries physical messages between locations that are within telepathic range. The job only still exists for bureaucratic reasons.6Guard. Stands places. Not actually capable of stopping anyone. Has never encountered an actual security threat and would not know what to do.





















































2d6Today’s rumor2THE PRINCESS IS HAVING ANOTHER PARTY! She does this every month.3Hey, there’s some weird foreigners here! This is you. You have 10 minutes to an hour before the guards show up.4Someone broke into one of the vaults over at [exact vault location] yesterday. 1-in-6 chance it’s another Aian interloper.5The Guard is looking for [powerful magical item], if anyone misplaced it! Someone had a really awful reason to borrow a very dangerous tool.6Someone really screwed up. A local jobber (see jobs table) had a problem.7Did you hear about [rabbit]‘s promotion? Banal, pointless gossip.8[Rabbit name] is in love with [guard name]! Both parties mortified.9Something fell from space recently! Meteor, dead bird, celestial horror.10The Archive is doing inventory today! 3-in-6: restricted areas are especially active and watched. Else, areas are totally unwatched & chaotic.11The Princess is angry! Everyone starts panicking & hyperventilating over anything that could possibly be a problem, especially strange foreigners.12Something strange last night happened in the sky! Meteor shower, visible volcanic eruption on Aia, perfectly clear night on the Moon.

Book-1---Glass-Empires/{-Appendix-A-}---.title: { Appendix A } - Mutations & Wounds
Book-1---Glass-Empires/{-Appendix-A-}---.content:
Mutations
When a character suffers a Mutation:

The GM rolls 1d6 to choose the target nature & effects of the Mutation, shifting down the list if the rolled result is inapplicable:

1–3: The mutating effect’s description or source, if any.
4–5: The characteristics of the victim themselves.
6: The characteristics of the immediate environment surrounding the victim.


The GM rolls twice on the Mutations & Wounds Table (p. @) to determine Location, and chooses the most thematic of the two rolled results, discarding the other.
The GM determines the nature & effects of the Mutation based on the words & location & writes a description for the new Fact representing it.

Mutations are never inherently lethal & they shouldn’t prohibit continued play for PCs, but they can be very unpleasant … or quite helpful.
Wounds
When you receive a Wound, generate its location by rolling on the Mutation Location table. If you don’t have the location in question, stop; you don’t receive a Wound! Otherwise, perform an unmodifed Wound Save (straight 2d6 with no modifiers).





























ResultWound SaveCrit≥12You don’t actually suffer a Wound. You’re fine!Pass8–11The body part is merely injured & is unusable until the Wound heals.Seven7You permanently lose half your capacity (i.e. weak/unsteady leg, half your breath).Failure3–6The body part is destroyed.Fumble≤2The body part is destroyed. Roll for a second Wound location.
If the body part of the Wound isn’t destroyed, then whenever you perform the Rest Move (p. @), cross-reference the Rest Threshold for a period longer than that, the Wound is healed.
Mutations & Wounds Table















































































































































d66BodyLocationRestThresholdIf Location isDestroyed, You…11Entire head1 monthDie.12Face1 weekDie.13Arms1 monthLose 1 arm.14Hands or whatever1 monthLose 1 hand/whatever.15Legs1 monthLose 1 leg.16Feet or whatever1 monthLose 1 foot/whatever.21−22Torso1 monthDie.23−24Entire body1 seasonDie.25−26Tail1 weekLose 1 tail.31Nose1 weekCannot smell.32Ears1 weekCannot hear.33Eyes1 monthCannot see.34Most thematic organ1 month5-in-6 chance of death.35−36Horns/other protrusions1 weekLose 1 horn.41−42Skin1 weekDie painfully.43−44Mouth, tongue, or breath1 monthDie.45−46Soul/aura1 weekDie.51−52Voice1 monthCannot speak.53−54Mind/personality1 seasonDie.55Wings1 monthLose 1 wing.56Genitals1 monthCan’t reproduce.61−66Gain additional (re-roll)——

If you roll “Gain additional…” while checking for a Wound, the Wound affects two or more parts; make a Wound Save for each part’s destruction separately. If you roll it for a Mutation, the Mutation adds an additional body part (if applicable).
If you don’t have the named body part, a Mutation will add it while a Wound will do nothing & be negated.

Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-4-}---Enco.title: { 4 } - Encounters & Locations @
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-4-}---Enco.content:
@ Locations
You should create a 1d6 random table of at least 6 distinct Locations for each distinct area, biome, or region in play. This table is used for small, hidden/secretive, or outwardly unremarkable landmarks or objects that are flexible in placement.


                  
                  Tip
                  
                

By not marking a location-independent Location ahead of time, you can minimize prep work required to start playing & simply regenerate the list as it gets used up.


Locations can be a variety of things, but are generally small, hidden/secretive, or outwardly unremarkable landmarks or objects. Examples may include but aren’t limited to small castles/forts, the homes of colorful NPCs, graveyards or battle sites, merchants or artisans of particular character, dungeons, secret treasures, ruins, potential settlement sites, or locations of intense immaterial value/significance to one or more existing worldly Factions.
You only need a new separate table for each area that has a wildly distinct character in terms of what can be found there (i.e. deserts vs. tundras vs. jungles). Major differences in cultures (i.e. cultures whose homes are built underground) may also justify the creation of a separate table.
Whenever the game requires that a new & unmarked Location be brought into play (i.e. as a consequence of the Travel Move), roll on your Locations table for that area to figure out what Location in question is the topic. From that point onward, the Location is marked as being at the spot where it was discovered. Remove it from your table & replace the entry with a new Location.
Generator: Random Locations
The GM may use this Location generator if he has no Random Locations table suitable for a given region, area, or biome, or to generate new entries to fill out a region-specific table.

Roll d66 on the Location table & then on the Location Status table, then determine Faction ownership.
If the location/status result is nonsense for the area & you can’t figure out how to marry it to the fiction, discard the Location. Nothing is found.

Random Locations may not be immediately actionable, but they always stick around. Mark them on the map after they’re discovered.

Determining Faction Ownership
Locations are generally owned. The GM decides one of the following on the spot based on narrative logic:

If only a single major Faction would plausibly claim this: It’s a Lv1d6 Subfaction of theirs.
If multiple Factions would or did fight over it: Assign odds to a d6 & roll to determine owner, contestants, etc.
If it’s too remote, worthless, hidden, or obscure: It’s a new independent Lv1d6 Faction.
If unsure: It’s abandoned.

Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-5-}---Runn.filePath: Book 2 - Gamemaster's Guide/{ 5 } - Running a Braunstein @.md
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-5-}---Runn.title: { 5 } - Running a Braunstein @
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-5-}---Runn.content:
@ Currently the Braunstein appendix, expanded. Periods, phases, diplomacy, order execution, conflict resolution, communication rules, multiple-GM coordination. This is its own campaign mode and needs dedicated treatment.

Glass Empires is built to be managed either as a traditional RPG campaign featuring a party of Players whose Party is based on the premise of working together to achieve a larger goal set out at the start of a campaign or as what is known as a Braunstein.
A Braunstein is an open-ended scenario featuring Player vs. Player conflict under a fog of war with simultaneous order execution in a scenario that pits participants against each other. The role of a GM (of which there may be more than one) in a Braunstein is to organize players, manage information & resolve conflicts in order execution while maintaining the world in a way that exacerbates the tensions & conflicts that forms the heart of a Braunstein.
What makes a Braunstein?

Conflict between players. The GM must engineer an initial scenario wherein players have a reason to interact & potentially fight over resolutions to their problems. A “hard” solution is to design a scenario wherein not all players can win; where the world is at least in some way zero-sum.
Asymmetric capabilities. Players who have variable assets have something to trade, and negotiation is the lifeblood of a game like this.

Setup
The GM should arrange for the following:

A public space wherein all Players may communicate with each other;
Private channels wherein Players may privately communicate with selected other Players;
A means of conveying announcements, such as the outcome of issued orders;
A selection of times wherein GMs can gather Players whose orders conflict in such a way that requires they be present at an ad-hoc session to resolve the conflict.

An instant messaging server & a virtual tabletop platform is perfect for both of these.
Player Communication
All Players may freely communicate with each other via direct conversations (in text or voice). No Player may provide proof of conversations or statements (i.e. via screenshot), although if both players agree, a written document can be created as an artifact as part of written orders submitted to the GM.
Periods & Phases
The game is played out as a series of Periods representing a certain amount of real-world & in-game time. By default, 1 Period is:

1 week of in-game time;
Up to 1 week in real-world time, depending on how long it takes for each Player to issue orders to the GM(s).

Phase Structure
There are two real-world Phases that occur during each Period: Diplomacy & Execution.
Diplomacy Phase
During the Diplomacy Phase, Players may scheme, communicate, and otherwise talk among each other in public or private. In addition, throughout this phase, each Player determines & issues private written orders for their actions during this Period to the GM.
Until the Phase ends, players can edit or override orders they previously set in.
Diplomacy ends when all Players issue orders or it has been 1 real-world week since the start of the Phase. Players who haven’t issued orders by the end of the Phase are assumed to do nothing other than defend themselves during the Execution Phase.


                  
                  Why can we communicate when our PCs shouldn't be able to? 
                  
                

Because players may always have communication with each other in order to facilitate role-playing & engagement in the game, the Diplomacy Phase does not necessarily correlate to actual conversations that happen in-game. Instead, this phase represents:


Public knowledge being shared, such as rumors or leaks about behaviors or events being shared between players through intermediaries such as courtiers, friends, or allies;
Past conversations held at an oppoirtunity
Strategic wargaming or intuitive reasoning performed by both sides not through talking but rather by reasoning out in private what the other members of the conversation would do in any given situation.

Figure whichever one suits the situation. The important part is that no orders, agreements, or material exchanges can happen during the Diplomacy Phase. Only (verbal, non-binding) agreements can be made, and in order for actions to be executed upon, each Player must explicitly issue orders to a GM that would facilitate those agreements or exchanges, such as by sending messengers to the other parties involved in the agreement(s). The only exception to this rule is if the Players’ PCs are in close in-game proximity to each other during the Diplomacy Phase, and even then, both must still issue orders to the GM explaining their actions in order to execute upon it.

Execution Phase
During the Execution Phase, the GM(s) analyze all written orders received during Diplomacy & determines what happens during the actual Period. In particular, the GM figures out if any of the Players’ orders results in a collision or potential point of conflict that must be resolved before the next Phase can begin.
The Execution Phase lasts as long as is required for the GM(s) to find all points of conflict that would occur during the in-game Period, bring the affected Players together in order to resolve those conflict(s) (i.e. via ad-hoc sessions to host battles or in-character discussions, text chats, or whatever other methods are required), & then declare the results of all orders to all parties that would have heard of them.
The Execution Phase ends as soon as the GM(s) are finished resolving all conflicts & issuing new information to the Players.
Order Formatting
Orders are a sequence of Moves that are undertaken by the Player’s PC over the course of the in-game Period. Players can only order their own PC to take actions. PCs can delegate via communication with Retainers (to order Subfactions) or courtiers (to order their directly-ruled Factions).
The total action economy for each PC is, therefore, down to how many Retainers/Factions they can issue orders to in-game in addition to their PC’s own personal time that can be dedicated towards a Move during the Period.
Players must provide a strict order of operations for their orders. Otherwise, orders will be executed in the order received by the GM.
Players may specify breaks or conditional reasoning based on the outcome of orders.
Other than for these rules, there is no strict formatting on what orders can be issued so long as the orders are coherent enough to be understood by the GM.
Special Conflict Cases

Two player-led armies march to same location — who has initiative? Is there a battle, or does one arrive first?

GM determines which army becomes aware of the other first, & during the execution phase provides this information to the Player in control of that army. The Player may make decisions based on that information within the scope of the conflict.
If no PC was directly leading an involved army, the GM has the ability to adjudicate how the NPCs in command react according to the data they receive at the time when they receive it.
They have a battle if one or both sides can force it & want to force it given the world scenario. The GM has the information to adjudicate an outcome.


Mutual assassination attempts

Strictly speaking, “assassination” isn’t something the game actually has rules for! This is a good point & something to fix.


A player wants to discover a piece of information & then decide what to do upon receiving it.

GM determines when during the in-game Period the PC would uncover the information, and then (after resolving any preceding conflicts) describes the scenario to the Player at the time of their discovery of the information. The GM may set a time limit for the Player to react beforehand.


Racing for the same NPC faction’s allegiance

Who gets there first? Who has the most compelling offer?


Spy/counter-spy collisions

Again, not something we really have rules for — infiltration. But it’d fall out similarly to the player-led army example.



Special Notes
The Negotiation rules (p. @) don’t apply when dealing with other PCs.
Written Contracts
Players can agree to create written contracts. In-game violation of contracts might Please or Anger NPC Factions or Subfactions who have an interest in the upholding of law (or in the stakes at play regarding the contract’s contents).
Asset Transfers
You have to literally issue orders to give another PC items. This might come down to a GM-resolved conflict or negotiation period.
Fog of War Rules
Much of this comes down to the GM determining what PCs would have the opportunity to observe or see during the Period’s events.

What if players want to negotiate or trade in-person?

They can arrange to meet up during Diplomacy, issue orders to do so, and then have a meeting (with or without GM mediation, depending on if anything happens in the negotiation that demands GM presence to resolve).


What do players learn about other players’ actions at period end?

“As much as they could observe, and nothing outside that unless the players in question choose to share it.”


How do spy networks/intelligence gathering work as orders?

Again, espionage is not something we’ve rules for, which is bad since it will come up literally instantly.


What triggers discovery of covert actions?

See above.


The Panopticon presumably gives Arcades special information — what exactly?

The controller of the Panopticon can see armies, towns, fleets, & castles as per the Hills visibility level across the entirety of Aia & much of the surrounding Rim ocean.



NPC Faction behavior
All Factions that aren’t PC-controlled are ordinarily managed by the GM. This includes Subfactions managed by PC Retainers. A GM may delegate decision-making for a PC’s Subfaction to that Player if the Subfaction in question is compliant or has no reason to rebel against that Player.

@ GM-facing rules on building custom Classes, Features, Origins, et cetera.
@ Custom Classes
@ Custom Magical Schools
Magicians mechanically consist of a Magical School possessing a mixture of Class Features & a Spell Tradition. The means & methods by which these are obtained varies.
In narrative terms, magicians have a philosophy that encompasses the way they see & interact with the world. The art of dealing with spirits is intrinsically tied to your mindset as a practitioner of magic.
Class Features
Magician Class Features tend to be double-edged swords relating to their nature in reality.
chosen via method:

Linear
Random
Choice of selection

Magical Traditions
Each magical school has a tradition of 12 spells based on the most fundamental aspects of how they interact with & view the world.
Traditions represent minor spirits; grease elementals, the spirit of an echo, a newborn god with nothing to offer. These spirits are common & easy to locate, or else they deliberately seek out magicians of the appropriate mentality & presentation.
Most magicians can capture & sell these traditional spells fairly easily. It takes about a month of effort. It is not a tremendously profitable market.
More complex, powerful & dangerous spells come from the beast markets or from hunting.

Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-Appendix-A.filePath: Book 2 - Gamemaster's Guide/{ Appendix A } - Design Essays @.md
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-Appendix-A.title: { Appendix A } - Design Essays @
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-Appendix-A.content:
@ A series of design essays that guided the development of Glass Empires.
The Existence of Literal Genies
In a world where gods:

Exist;
Have limited time & attention;
Can be contacted from anywhere;
Have many worshipers making frivolous pleas;
Require those worshipers to stay loyal for any reason;

Gods will be blamed for failing to show up when a petitioner is in need. This can potentially cascade & prohibit gods of exceptional power whose rituals allow perfectly unambiguous contact & information transmission methods to become increasingly at risk against smaller, more local competitors who can serve needs quickly, accurately & at length.
For example, imagine Zeus as the Father of all Gods being pulled every which way across the Mediterranean, especially if we assume he physically exists & cannot manifest on-the-spot with fast-as-light movement. If he communicated by literally manifesting before you & saying “No,” he would be at a disadvantage because it would be very obvious whenever he didn’t show up. Humans are far more spiteful over a clear failure or refusal than for a miscommunication or ambiguity.
The peasant cannot see the volume of requests & worldy complexity that Zeus faces. What he thinks instead is: “I cannot reach Zeus. I sacrificed that goat on his altar for NOTHING. I have been ROBBED.” He becomes a very angry customer.
As a result, gods may deliberately introduce contact rituals that are opaque & noisy so that interpretation becomes a difficult art. This minimizes points of contact & potentially creates false signals which works in favor of confirmation bias. A 50% false signal feels like only a 25% error rate, assuming that a false signal is correct 50% of the time — & humans are exceptionally bad at handling or understanding probabilistic outcomes.
A Simplified Economic Trade Model
Rather than emulate the inputs & outputs of every Location in the game & their necessary demands & productive centers, we can observe an essential tenant key to any population: Everyone must be economically sustainable.
There must be inputs & outputs. Furthermore, traders will optimize for functionality.
If we assume that the system is stable & functional — which is a big caveat, especially during times of war & unrest — then we can simplify trade into something resembling the following approximate structure:

You buy Trade Goods at a Settlement. You have a target Settlement in mind.
If sold at that place, it produces a random but usually profitable profit margin based on distance.
If sold elsewhere, it produces a random profit margin that is weighted lower, such that it is usually not profitable accounting for risk.

Consider that this system has at least the following flaws:

You can’t capture anomalies in production level.
You can’t capture disruptions in other risk factors that don’t directly relate to distance. What if the only route is exceptionally dangerous? What if it’s safe & stable?
You can’t capture obvious similarities in inputs/outputs. What if a village has identical needs to the original destination?

Simplifications fail to capture edge cases as per usual. (We might note that arguably, the areas where a simplification fails are more interesting than anything else.) In any case, I believed that this framework is only suitable for a game where detailed economic & trade warfare is not expected to play a prominent role — in fact, that it may be best left out altogether, then, to avoid distracting players with the promise of something that isn’t meant to be a meaningful component. Why over-complicate matters?
Glass Empires’ Faction System Only Works for Pre-industrial Societies
The only reason the implicit economic simulation in Glass Empires functions is because we have made massive simplifications based on an implicit model of human society. Most critically, we assume that human population has a tight relationship to economic productivity. This is the key assumption that the Industrial Age rips to shreds.
In addition, we assume that population centers correspond tightly to power, which is why we can get away with assuming a Capital’s population is reflective of the rest of its power. This is true for almost all of history but decouples more extensively the more industrialized a state becomes.
Our assumptions are also dependent on a relatively inefficient tribal power structure. And in addition to real-world tribal & factional factors, Aia has several compounding confounding factors that prohibit centralization of power:

Absence of true firearms keeps cheap stone fortifications viable
Highly decentralized mail & courier service courtesy of the local bird-brains prohibits rulers from monopolizing high-speed communications;
Hostile & dangerous wilderness teeming with inexplicable & deadly magic provides continual “shatterzone” pressures from nearly every wild region;
Realms artificially sustained through a single ruler’s combat & magical prowess often struggles to survive a succession crisis;
And more still!

In the context of Aia, none of this is a problem. The Grand Disc is not yet near an industrial revolution … although if you ran out the clock another couple hundred years, it might get there. But if you set about deploying the Glass Empires ruleset in another setting, be careful of the assumptions you import.
It is worth keeping in mind as a GM that the Lunarians (p. @) do not operate on a clear-cut economy at all & that attempting to model their existence using the Faction system is a terribly flawed route to pursue. They are rather like an odd endgame bonus stage, in this sense.

Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-Appendix-B.filePath: Book 2 - Gamemaster's Guide/{ Appendix B } - The Moon.md
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-Appendix-B.title: { Appendix B } - The Moon
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-Appendix-B.content:
Getting to the Moon

Kill a Moon Rabbit Operator, steal its Rabbit Guard’s Feet Feature with alchemy, and then jump to the Moon. This is extremely risky & is usually a one-way trip because the Moon is under perpetual cloud cover. Once you pass through the clouds, Aia isn’t visible until you can climb to the top of a Lunarian crystal spire or one of the mountains that penetrates the cloud cover, which means you can’t return even if you brought a spare to return with.
Find a Lunarian Wormhole & pass through it, and then escape detection & expulsion by the Lunarian Royal Guard. This is extremely risky because the Lunarians keep extremely tight watch over every wormhole they operate specifically to prevent Aian creatures from slipping through.
Build a magical contraption & fly to the Moon. Not impossible, but very unlikely & difficult. I don’t really know what kind of engineering would be required to achieve this. It’s certainly beyond any mortal ability.
Magic. A sufficiently powerful spirit of movement could likely teleport you to the Moon in a manner not dissimilar to the moon rabbit operators.

Leaving the Moon

Tell the Lunarians you’re there. If the Lunarian Royal Family learns that an Aian made it to the Moon, they will happily strip you of everything from the Moon you possess & kick you butt-naked out of one of their wormholes to freeze to death in a distant wilderness. It’s not a dignified or easy method of exit, but they will let you go.
Kill a Moon Rabbit Operator, steal its lunar hop & climb a crystal spire. The Lunarian towers that penetrate the cloud cover are the launching points for rabbit operators that get deployed to Aia. During the lunar apex, anyone that can match its hop can reach any point on Aia.

Lunar Landscape
Aia’s Moon is a strange & unpleasant landscape for anything not born there.
Half the Moon is blanketed in a nearly perpetual night, as the Moon is situated further from Aia than the Sun is, and therefore never sees light on the side facing away from Aia. Even if you’re not on the dark side of the Moon, the entire Moon has a nearly-constant cloud cover that rarely dissipates, granting only very brief glimpses of direct sunlight.
There is an extremely powerful & omnipresent curvature visible in all directions, because the Moon is only ~300 miles in diameter. This creates a “claustrophobic horizon” effect. You can only see around half a mile into the distance on flat terrain, around 40 times shorter than on Aia’s surface (where on a clear day, one can see for maybe 20 miles). Everywhere you stand, it always feels like you’re standing on a very gentle hill, even if the ground is level. For similar reasons, flat maps are almost totally useless beyond an extremely local scale, because distortions grow so rapidly.
The entire Moon essentially shares a single weather system. When it rains on the Moon, it rains everywhere. The ~280,000 square miles of terrain pegs the Moon at around the size of Texas, or France plus the U.K. combined.
There is no consistent method of keeping time. Without the Sun orbiting in a regular manner & with the omnipresent cloud cover, the entire surface is perpetually dark, with only occasional breaks in the sky on the bright side of the moon. Any reference to “days” is purely for convenience’s sake.
And that’s not even getting into the geography, flora, or fauna.
Lunarian Kingdom
Ludicrously elitist towards anyone on the surface world. Absolutely not better than them in any way, except that they actually are. Lunarians are beautiful, intelligent, highly developed, mostly ignorant to the world beyond themselves, & kept in a developmental stasis by their rulers, who have no desire to ever allow the Moon to change in any meaningful way.
Lunarian Royal Family
The Lunarian Royal Family consists of the Lunarian Princess & her direct family. They are all immortal, terrifyingly deadly, & essentially content to stay in their palace.
They have layers & layers of reactive defenses of every sort that can be imagined & many of those that cannot. They have Continuity of Governance protocols that extend back to the creation of the Moon. They have plans for every possible contingency. They are hell-bent on things staying exactly the way they are within their bubble and will brook no competition nor challenge that will disrupt them in any way.
And yet, they have weaknesses.

Passivity: Most of their defenses are based on passive detection. They make no personal effort to protect themselves.
Aian Ignorance: They cannot and do not pay attention to most of the Grand Disc. They assume that no meaningful force can or would fly from Aia to the Disc without Lunarian magic/wormholes.

They watch wormhole usage closely for intruders, but have no tracking for celestial object entry & exits, in part because birds & some other creatures regularly visit the Moon.


Novel Magic: They usually rely on broad-spectrum anti-magic effects rather than specific anti-magic effects in part because of the difficulty of predicting magical evolution.

Despite this, their ability to deploy deadly troops (albeit somewhat incompetent ones, & in small numbers) to anywhere on Aia instantaneously combined with their enormous magical & technological advantages means that they could freely manipulate the state of Aia on a whim if they chose to do so. (Some say that the Lunarian Princess drove Kain’s wife away in order to drive him from the throne of Glass.)
Practical role in a game
The Lunarians have a stockpile of enormously powerful & dangerous magical weaponry & utilities that could turn the Grand Disc on its head if even a handful were brought back to the surface. Going to the Moon & coming back is something that Lv10+ PCs would do in order to supercharge their conquests with world-wrecking power at the risk of bringing down the wrath of aeons-old proto-gods onto their heads.
Alternatively, much of the Moon is not actually under Lunarian occupation as it consists of a bizarre & deadly alien landscape with a variety of interesting rewards that by themselves could be extraordinarily valuable even if they didn’t change the fate of empires (merely kingdoms).

index.title: Glass Empires SRD
index.content:
Glass Empires
Glass Empires is a tabletop RPG about pre-industrial power dynamics on an animistic discworld — faction-building, debt, rulership, and the nature of the divine. It is OSR-adjacent but not a dungeon-crawl game. Your Level is your Followers. Your Faction is more important than your character.


                  
                  Danger
                  
                

This SRD is a live draft and reference document for an ongoing Braunstein campaign. Sections marked with @ are incomplete! Refer to Saki for clarification on any details.


The navigation in the sidebar lists all chapters.
